did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning with transfer learning (specifically AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet-18) to classify subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC), a skin cancer, from histological images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the design and validation of a deep learning software for classifying basal cell carcinoma subtypes using transfer learning (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet-18) on a dataset of 2249 image patches. It includes specific methods, results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC), and statistical analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep learning with transfer learning in pathology. Case study: classification of basal cell carcinoma
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning software to classify 10 subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from histological images using transfer learning with AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet-18. The dataset comprised 2,249 image patches labeled by three pathologists from 102 consecutive BCC cases at the Clinical Municipal Hospital in Cluj-Napoca, Romania. The models were trained on 90% of the data and tested on 10% across 100 independent sequences, achieving mean accuracies of 82.53% and AUCs of 0.99. Validation on an independent 50-image dataset showed results comparable to pathologist agreement, with best individual network accuracies exceeding 95%. The findings demonstrate that transfer learning can effectively assist in BCC subtype classification, supporting pathologist diagnosis and education.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on classifying 10 specific subtypes of basal cell carcinoma. The dataset comprised 2249 image patches from 102 consecutive BCC cases.
Three high-risk (HR) subtypes were included in the 10-class classification task. Counts: HR infiltrating and sclerosing (384), HR micronodular (310), HR superficial multifocal (14).

Seven low-risk (LR) subtypes were included in the 10-class classification task. Counts: LR with adnexal differentiation (78), LR nodular (508), LR nodular adenoid variant (218), LR nodular keratotic variant (101), LR nodular nodulocystic variant (126), LR pigmented (366), LR superficial unifocal (127).

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to 'classify 10 subtypes of BCC' from histological images. The task is a 10-class multiclass classification problem distinguishing between specific histological subtypes (e.g., HR infiltrating and sclerosing, LR nodular, etc.). Performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Clinical Municipal Hospital BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset comprising 2249 image patches (crops of 512x512 pixels) extracted from 102 consecutive basal cell carcinoma (BCC) cases. The tissue samples were surgically excised, processed with Hematoxylin-Eosin (HE) staining, and scanned at the Clinical Municipal Hospital in Cluj-Napoca, Romania. The patches were selected by a trained pathologist and a panel of three pathologists, keeping only those where the diagnosis was fully agreed upon.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet-18) via tenfold cross-validation methodology, where 90% of the data was used for training in each sequence.
use.validation: Used for validation during the tenfold cross-validation process (10% of the data kept for validation in each sequence).
use.testing: Used for testing the models (10% of the data kept for testing in each sequence) across 100 independent training sequences.
use.other: The dataset was also used to create a standalone application for live image classification.

datasets_used.name: Independent Validation Dataset
datasets_used.description: A separate, independent dataset of 50 histological images used to validate the software's performance against the agreement of three pathologists.
classes_used: Basal cell carcinoma subtypes (unspecified distribution in text)

use.testing: Used for external validation of the standalone application, with results compared to the opinion of three pathologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the dataset into training (90%) and validation/testing (10%) sets across 100 independent sequences to ensure robust model evaluation.
To assess the normality of the distribution of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC data samples.
To assess the normality of the distribution of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC data samples.
To assess the normality of the distribution of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC data samples.
To compare the mean accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC among the three deep learning models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet-18) to determine if there were significant differences.
To perform pairwise comparisons between the models following significant ANOVA results to identify specific differences in mean accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.
To measure the agreement between the deep learning networks and the three pathologists, correcting for chance agreement.
To summarize the central tendency and dispersion of performance metrics (ACC, SN, SP, AUC) across the 100 independent runs.
To visualize and analyze the classification performance, including true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class.

hypothesis_testing.test: One-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) followed by Post-hoc Tukey’s test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no significant differences between the mean performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, or AUC) of the three deep learning models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet-18).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant differences between the mean performance metrics of at least two of the three deep learning models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: For Accuracy and Sensitivity, the ANOVA p-values were 0.06 and 0.129 respectively (both > 0.05), leading to the conclusion that there were no significant differences between the models. For Specificity, the ANOVA p-value was 0.03 (< 0.05), indicating significant differences, which were further analyzed via Tukey's test. For AUC, the ANOVA p-value was <0.001 (< 0.05), indicating significant differences, with Tukey's test showing GoogLeNet and AlexNet significantly outperformed ResNet-18.

Clinical Municipal Hospital BCC Dataset.notes: ANOVA p-value for AUC was <0.001 (significant difference). Post-hoc Tukey's test p-values: AlexNet vs GoogLeNet 0.08, AlexNet vs ResNet-18 0.01, GoogLeNet vs ResNet-18 0.001. GoogLeNet and AlexNet significantly outperformed ResNet-18.
Results for the best individual network run for each architecture tested on the entire dataset.
Performance on High Risk (HR) vs Low Risk (LR) classification using the best performing network of each architecture.

Independent Validation Dataset (50 image.notes: The software was validated on 50 new images. Results were compared to the opinion of three pathologists using Cohen's kappa coefficient. The paper states the results are comparable to the pathologists' agreement but does not report specific kappa values or accuracy percentages for this specific 50-image set in the text provided.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset for 1000 classes

AlexNet.layers: 5 convolutional layers (followed by max-pooling), 2 fully connected layers

GoogLeNet.type: Convolutional Neural Network with Inception architecture

ResNet-18.type: Convolutional Neural Network with non-linear architecture

training_details.validation_method: Tenfold cross-validation (90% training, 10% validation/testing) across 100 independent sequences

classification_or_output_head.modification: Final fully connected layer and output layer replaced
classification_or_output_head.activation: Softmax (implied by original architecture and classification task)

preprocessing_techniques: Image cropping: Crops of 643x643 pixels were selected from whole-slide images, and only the central 512x512 pixels were used for the dataset.
Data augmentation: Additional pixels from the 643x643 crops permitted image rotation for data augmentation.
Quality control/Labeling: A trained pathologist and a panel of three pathologists selected crops, keeping only those where the diagnosis was fully agreed upon.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Additional pixels from the 643x643 image crops (specifically the border pixels outside the central 512x512 region used for the dataset) were utilized to permit image rotation for data augmentation.

conclusions: Deep learning with transfer learning using AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet-18 can effectively classify 10 subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from histological images.
The three deep learning architectures demonstrated comparable classification accuracies, indicating they reached a maximum classification rate on the dataset.
While there were no significant differences in mean accuracy or sensitivity among the models, GoogLeNet and AlexNet significantly outperformed ResNet-18 in terms of Area Under the Curve (AUC).
The software's performance on an independent validation dataset of 50 images was comparable to the agreement of three pathologists.
The developed software shows promising results and, with further development, can be successfully used to assist pathologists' diagnosis and teaching.
The study highlights the potential of AI to aid in the challenging task of establishing BCC subtypes, which impacts treatment management and recurrence risk assessment.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a lack of clinical context; the algorithm relies solely on histological images without considering patient history, family history, or other paraclinical tests, which may introduce diagnostic bias.
The dataset is considered small for the specific task and is unbalanced due to the natural distribution of BCC subtypes (e.g., HR superficial multifocal had only 14 samples vs. LR nodular with 508).
Data collection was significantly impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and social distancing restrictions, which drastically reduced the number of operated BCC cases available at the hospital.
The dataset is derived from a single center (Clinical Municipal Hospital, Cluj-Napoca, Romania), limiting generalizability and potentially introducing population or dataset bias.
The study acknowledges that smaller datasets tend to perform poorly compared to larger ones, despite having an easier data collection process.
The independent validation was performed on a very small dataset of only 50 images, which may not robustly represent real-world performance.
The software was developed as a standalone application for educational and diagnostic assistance, but its deployment in clinical practice requires further development and validation.
